title: Stage Musical Two-Act Structure
author: (theatrical convention)
source: Theatrical-exhibition convention; structure bent around the practical need for an interval. The Act I 'button' and the Act II '11 o'clock number' are the canonical landmarks.

lineage.note: Form following the building, not the story — the act structure exists because the audience needs to use the bathroom. But the conventions (the strong Act I button, the late Act II low point) are real structural lessons.

stages.name: Normal World
stages.function: Opening number establishes setting and ensemble.

stages.name: Inciting Incident
stages.function: The 'I want' song; the disruption that launches the story.

stages.name: Point of No Return
stages.function: Commitment; the Act I closing 'button' that sends audience to intermission wanting more.

stages.name: Intermission
stages.function: Audience break. The cliff-hanger has 15 minutes to land.

stages.name: Midpoint Resumes
stages.function: Act II opener; restate stakes, reorient the audience.

stages.name: The Big Gloom
stages.function: All-is-lost moment; the late Act II low point. Often the '11 o'clock number.'

stages.name: Climax into Resolution
stages.function: Final confrontation; the answer to the central question.

applicability.limitations: Built around an exhibition convention that mostly doesn't apply to documentary distribution. The Act I button is still useful as a structural test ('does Act I end on a hook?').
